findings.portOrUsageNotes:
SDK is the CLIENT-facing shell, not the engine; the orchestrator (mcp-server module) drives it like this:

1) Construct: `const mcp = new McpServer({ name:'pi-workflows', version:'x' }, { capabilities:{ tools:{}, logging:{} } });` (capabilities optional — registerTool auto-registers tools.listChanged; declare `logging:{}` only if you call sendLoggingMessage, else assertNotificationCapability throws).

2) Register the workflow tool with a ZOD RAW SHAPE (object of zod schemas, NOT z.object()):
```
mcp.registerTool('workflow', {
  title:'Run a dynamic workflow', description:'...',
  inputSchema: {
    script: z.string(),                                   // required
    args: z.record(z.string(), z.unknown()).optional(),
    maxAgents: z.number().int().positive().max(1000).default(1000).optional(),
    concurrency: z.number().int().positive().max(16).optional(),
    agentRetries: z.number().int().min(0).max(3).optional(),
    agentTimeoutMs: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
    tokenBudget: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
    resumeFromRunId: z.string().optional(),
  },
  // outputSchema optional; if set, handler MUST return structuredContent matching it
}, async (args, extra) => { /* run engine synchronously */ });
```
Validation is automatic (mcp.js:166 validateToolInput) — invalid args → JSON-RPC InvalidParams BEFORE your handler runs, so clamping (concurrency<=16, agentRetries<=3) belongs in the schema; defaults (maxAgents=1000) via .default(). Note: with zod .default(), the value is applied during parse so args.maxAgents is always present in the handler.

3) Connect: `await mcp.connect(new StdioServerTransport());` — stdout is RESERVED for JSON-RPC; route all engine/agent logs to stderr or mcp.server.sendLoggingMessage(...). Never console.log to stdout.

4) Synchronous run + progress: inside the handler, read `const pt = extra._meta?.progressToken;` then as the engine advances call `if (pt!==undefined) await extra.sendNotification({ method:'notifications/progress', params:{ progressToken: pt, progress: done, total: planned, message:'...' } });`. The tools/call response is not sent until your handler resolves, so the run is fully synchronous from the client's perspective; progress is the only in-flight signal. Wire `extra.signal` (AbortSignal) into the engine for cancellation/agentTimeoutMs.

5) Final result: `return { content:[{ type:'text', text: humanSummary }], structuredContent: runResultObject };` (structuredContent REQUIRED iff outputSchema declared). Throwing inside the handler is caught and returned as {isError:true} text — for hard failures you may prefer that, but structured failure data should go in structuredContent/isError.

6) resumeFromRunId is purely an inputSchema field you read in the handler and pass to the engine's journal/resume — the SDK has no resume concept; it is a normal arg.

7) checkpoint()/elicitation: call `await mcp.server.elicitInput({ message, requestedSchema:{ type:'object', properties:{ approve:{type:'boolean'} }, required:['approve'] } })` → `{action,content}`. Gated on the connected host advertising elicitation.form capability; if absent it THROWS — checkpoint() must catch and fall back (e.g., auto-approve or fail). requestedSchema allows only flat primitive properties (PrimitiveSchemaDefinition), no nesting.
findings.seamFacts:
Constraints this imposes on the workflow tool input/output envelope and the run/progress/AgentRunner model:

INPUT ENVELOPE: The tool inputSchema is a Zod raw shape (Record<string, ZodSchema>; zod ^3.25||^4.0). All eight params (script required string; args, maxAgents, concurrency, agentRetries, agentTimeoutMs, tokenBudget, resumeFromRunId optional) live here. Bounds (concurrency<=16 via .max(16), agentRetries<=3, maxAgents default 1000) are enforced by Zod at parse time, before the handler — failures surface as JSON-RPC InvalidParams, not as workflow errors. `arguments` over the wire is Record<string,unknown> (types.js:1336); the SDK parses it into typed args. There is no nesting limit on the tool inputSchema (that limit is only for elicitation requestedSchema).

OUTPUT ENVELOPE: Final result MUST conform to CallToolResult = { content: ContentBlock[] (defaults []), structuredContent?: object, isError?: boolean }. If the tool declares an outputSchema, structuredContent is MANDATORY and SDK-validated; otherwise content (e.g. one text block) carries the result. This means the shared run-result type produced by workflow-engine should serialize cleanly to a JSON object for structuredContent AND/OR a text summary. Engine-level failures should map to isError:true (the SDK auto-wraps thrown errors that way).

SYNCHRONOUS-RUN MODEL: One tools/call == one full workflow run. The handler (ToolCallback) is awaited to completion before the response is emitted; there is no built-in streaming of the result. The only in-flight channel is notifications/progress. This forces the AgentRunner seam (run(prompt,opts)->result) and the engine to be driven from inside this single async handler invocation; the handler must thread extra (progress sink + AbortSignal) down to the engine. The SDK's experimental Tasks API exists but registerTool uses taskSupport:'forbidden' (mcp.js:694/704) — do NOT rely on tasks for resume; resumeFromRunId is engine-owned.

PROGRESS MODEL: progress flows only if the client put _meta.progressToken on the tools/call. Server reads it at extra._meta.progressToken and emits {method:'notifications/progress', params:{progressToken, progress, total?, message?}} via extra.sendNotification (auto-correlated by relatedRequestId). progress is a monotonically increasing number; total optional; message optional. No server capability needed for progress. The seam should expose a progress callback (e.g. opts.onProgress(progress,total,message)) that the shell maps onto sendNotification.

CANCELLATION: extra.signal (AbortSignal) is the cancellation primitive; client tools/call cancellation aborts it. The AgentRunner/engine seam should accept an AbortSignal (and derive per-agent timeouts from agentTimeoutMs) so cancellation/timeout propagate to in-flight ACP agents.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP: checkpoint() maps to mcp.server.elicitInput (form mode). It is a server→client request requiring host elicitation.form capability and is awaited inline within the synchronous run. It returns {action:'accept'|'decline'|'cancel', content?}. The shared types should model checkpoint as an async call returning that tri-state, with a fallback when the host lacks elicitation. requestedSchema is restricted to flat primitive properties.

SERVER OBJECT: McpServer is high-level; advanced calls (elicitInput, raw notification, sendLoggingMessage, createMessage) are on mcp.server (the underlying Server). The shell must hold a reference to mcp.server. stdout is exclusively JSON-RPC — logging seam must avoid stdout.
discrepancies: No file:line drift to report against the SDK: the repo README (the orchestrator design doc, /home/vikash/agentprism-workflows/README.md) references @modelcontextprotocol/sdk ONLY by name/concept (l17 'registerTool', l120/l206 'notifications/progress', l147 'stdio MCP server registering the workflow tool', l158-161 dep pin, l214-216 elicitation→checkpoint). It cites NO specific SDK file:line, so there is nothing to cross-verify line-by-line for this surface. The file:line citations at README l453-475 belong to codex-acp/claude-agent-acp, not this SDK.
README l17/l147 says the tool is registered with `registerTool` — matches SDK mcp.d.ts:150 / mcp.js:699. Confirmed, no drift. (The bare `tool()` method exists but is @deprecated, mcp.d.ts:112-146.)

README l214-216 claim checkpoint() 'falls back to ... elicitation' — VERIFIED that elicitInput exists (index.d.ts:158) BUT it is hard-gated on the connected client advertising elicitation.form capability and THROWS otherwise (index.js:158-161, 362-363). The README's 'falls back to elicitation' is only true when the MCP host supports elicitation; over a host lacking it, checkpoint() cannot use elicitation and needs its own fallback. Flag for Phase 1.

README l199 says concurrency is 'clamped to 16' and l200 agentRetries '≤3'. The SDK does not clamp; it only validates whatever Zod schema you supply. To realize README semantics you must encode .max(16)/.max(3) (hard reject) OR clamp inside the handler (silent clamp) — these are different behaviors; the README wording ('clamped') implies silent clamp, which Zod .max() does NOT do (it rejects). Decide in Phase 1.
openQuestions: Should out-of-bounds concurrency/agentRetries be REJECTED (Zod .max → InvalidParams) or silently CLAMPED (handler-side)? README says 'clamped to 16' which implies clamp, not reject — affects whether bounds live in the Zod schema or the handler.

Should the workflow tool declare an outputSchema (forcing structuredContent + SDK validation of the run-result shape) or return only a text content block? This depends on the shared run-result type Phase 1 freezes.

Does the target MCP host (Claude Code / Zed) actually advertise the `elicitation`/`elicitation.form` capability over stdio? If not, checkpoint() over MCP is unavailable and needs a concrete fallback (auto-approve, fail, or defer).

Will clients reliably send _meta.progressToken on the workflow tools/call? If a host omits it, progress notifications cannot be correlated — confirm Claude Code sets progressToken so the synchronous-run progress UX works.
resumeFromRunId is engine-owned (SDK has no resume); confirm the engine journal/run-id format that the shell passes through unchanged.

experimental Tasks API (call-now/fetch-later) is available but registerTool forces taskSupport:'forbidden'. Confirm the synchronous model (single long tools/call) is acceptable for long multi-agent runs vs. needing the Tasks pattern for very long runs that may exceed client request timeouts.

agentRunnerInterfaceInputs: The engine references the runner by ONE method only: `agent?: Pick<WorkflowAgent,"run">` (workflow.ts:59, verified exact). So AgentRunner = { run(prompt, opts): Promise<result> } — a single-method interface, nothing else.

There is exactly ONE call site: `agentRunner.run(prompt, {...} as any)` at workflow.ts:465, inside the limiter thunk inside the agent() global, wrapped by withTimeout(promise, timeout, label). workflow.ts:283 (`const agentRunner = options.agent ?? new WorkflowAgent(options)`) is the BINDING, not the call.

Signature (agent.ts:332, verified): `run<TSchemaDef extends TSchema|undefined=undefined>(prompt: string, options: AgentRunOptions<TSchemaDef> = {}): Promise<AgentRunResult<TSchemaDef>>`. prompt is a plain positional string; opts is a single object; opts is optional (defaults to {}).

The opts the engine actually passes (workflow.ts:465-488, verified, cast `as any`) are 14 fields: label, schema, signal, instructions, model, tier, toolNames, disallowedToolNames, cwd, onModelResolved, onModelFallback, onUsage, onHistory. The engine does NOT pass `tools` or `maxSchemaRetries` even though AgentRunOptions declares them.

Return is the RAW value, not a wrapper: schema present => Static<schema> (parsed/validated object); no schema => assistant text string (AgentRunResult, agent.ts:286-288, verified). There is NO {result,usage} envelope.

Usage is delivered OUT-OF-BAND via the onUsage(AgentUsage) callback, never via the return value (workflow.ts:482-484 captures it into a closure var; agent.ts:441-455 fires onUsage on BOTH success and error paths before disposal). total===0 means the provider reported nothing and the engine falls back to a chars/4 estimate.
The return value must be JSON-serializable and stable for a fixed identity: it is journaled verbatim via onAgentJournal({index,hash,result}) at workflow.ts:502 and replayed on resume at :413.
Empty-output guard applies to NO-schema calls only: a whitespace/empty string result makes the engine throw AGENT_EMPTY_OUTPUT (recoverable) at workflow.ts:494 (isEmptyTextAgentResult, :1085).

Error contract: run() should THROW; the engine wraps via wrapError into a WorkflowError with .code + .recoverable (+agentLabel, +resetHint). recoverable=true => retried up to agentRetries (retryAttempts+1) then returns null; recoverable=false => rethrown and halts the run (incl. inside parallel/pipeline). Leaf-raised codes: AGENT_EMPTY_OUTPUT (recoverable), SCHEMA_NONCOMPLIANCE (non-recoverable), PROVIDER_USAGE_LIMIT (non-recoverable, carries resetHint -> engine pauses run as 'paused' not 'failed').

TIMEOUT and ABORT are the ENGINE's responsibility, not the runner's: withTimeout (workflow.ts:464, :1106) races a setTimeout throwing AGENT_TIMEOUT (recoverable); the engine passes opts.signal and calls throwIfAborted() before/after. The runner SHOULD honor opts.signal (wire to the underlying session cancel) but need NOT implement its own timeout.

Resume identity = hashAgentCall = sha256(JSON{prompt, model, tier, phase, agentType, agentDef, schema}) (workflow.ts:1045-1064). cwd, label, and instructions are NOT in the hash. callIndex is assigned at lexical call time before the limiter (workflow.ts:391), so the runner must NOT depend on invocation/completion order for its identity; nondeterminism of the LLM is masked by journaling, not by the runner.

checkpoint() is NOT a runner method — it spends no tokens, is gated on agentCount+abort, and journals/replays by callIndex; human-in-the-loop is the engine's options.confirm callback (workflow.ts:798-829). The AgentRunner interface needs NO checkpoint method.
Engine-level caps live in config.ts, not the runner: MAX_AGENTS_PER_RUN=1000, MAX_CONCURRENCY=16, MAX_AGENT_RETRIES=3, DEFAULT_AGENT_TIMEOUT_MS=null.

cwd flow: engine computes runCwd = worktree?.isolated ? worktree.cwd : undefined (workflow.ts:437) from createWorktree(baseCwd, `${runId}-${callIndex}-${label}`) and passes it as opts.cwd; the runner binds its session/tools to that cwd (maps to ACP session/new {cwd}, which must be an absolute path).
sharedTypeInputs: AgentRunOptions<TSchemaDef> (agent.ts:234-284, verified) fields: label?, schema?:TSchemaDef, tools?:ToolDefinition[], instructions?, signal?:AbortSignal, onUsage?:(AgentUsage)=>void, model?:string, tier?:string, onModelResolved?:(id)=>void, onModelFallback?:(spec)=>void, onHistory?:(AgentHistoryEntry[])=>void, cwd?, toolNames?:string[], disallowedToolNames?:string[], maxSchemaRetries?:number (default 2).

AgentUsage (agent.ts:225-232, verified) = { input:number; output:number; cacheRead:number; cacheWrite:number; total:number; cost:number }. All numbers; total===0 is the 'provider reported nothing' sentinel.

AgentResult / AgentRunResult<S> = S extends TSchema ? Static<S> : string (agent.ts:286-288, verified). The shared result type is a union of structured-object and plain string; usage is NOT part of it.

schema is a typebox TSchema today (imported at workflow.ts:5 and agent.ts:14). It is threaded into run() opts AND JSON.stringify'd into the resume hash (workflow.ts:1062) — changing the schema representation changes journal/resume compatibility.

Usage maps onto ACP PromptResponse.usage {totalTokens,inputTokens,outputTokens,thoughtTokens?,cachedReadTokens?,cachedWriteTokens?} (verified at types.gen.d.ts:2943/2984, marked UNSTABLE/experimental) + cost (Claude only; Codex has no cost). The Usage type must tolerate usage===undefined since ACP usage is experimental/optional.

ACP StopReason = end_turn | max_tokens | max_turn_requests | refusal | cancelled (verified types.gen.d.ts:2974) — the runner maps these to either a normal return or a thrown recoverable/non-recoverable WorkflowError; 'cancelled' is the post-abort value.

ACP core has ZERO native structured output (verified: grep of all dist/*.d.ts for outputSchema|structuredContent|json_schema = 0 matches). PromptResponse carries only stopReason + optional usage (no content/text field) — assistant text arrives only via streamed session/update agent_message_chunk. Therefore schema enforcement and result text assembly are CLIENT-SIDE concerns of acp-agents, not ACP.

Workflow MCP tool INPUT envelope is a Zod raw shape validated at parse time (InvalidParams before the handler runs): script (required string), args?, maxAgents? (default 1000), concurrency? (<=16), agentRetries? (<=3), agentTimeoutMs?, tokenBudget?, resumeFromRunId?. These mirror WorkflowManager.runSync ExecOptions {resumeJournal, maxAgents, tokenBudget, concurrency, agentRetries, confirm, onProgress}.

Workflow MCP tool OUTPUT envelope = CallToolResult { content: ContentBlock[] (default []); structuredContent?: object; isError? } (verified types.js:1289). If the tool declares an outputSchema, structuredContent is mandatory and SDK-validated. Thrown handler errors are auto-wrapped as {isError:true, content:[text]}.

Run model is SYNCHRONOUS: one tools/call == one full workflow run, awaited to completion before the response is emitted (registerTool uses taskSupport:'forbidden'). The only in-flight channel is notifications/progress, which requires the client to have sent _meta.progressToken; extra.signal (AbortSignal) is the cancellation primitive to thread into the engine.

PersistedRunState.journal = Array<{index:number; hash:string; result:unknown}>; RunStatus = pending|running|paused|completed|failed|aborted (run-persistence.ts:11,30-62). The frozen AgentResult must round-trip through this journal (JSON) unchanged for resume.

Where the shared types must live: AgentUsage, AgentHistoryEntry, AgentRunOptions, AgentRunResult currently sit in agent.ts (the only Pi-importing leaf). They must move to a shared-types package imported by BOTH workflow-engine and acp-agents so the engine never depends on acp-agents.

Claude structured result is reachable ONLY off the raw _claude/sdkMessage ext-notification as SDKResultSuccess.structured_output (typed unknown, sdk.d.ts:3983); the claude-agent-acp adapter never reads it. outputFormat is init-only (no Query.setOutputFormat). Codex turn-level outputSchema field EXISTS on v2 TurnStartParams (line 45) but the codex-acp adapter NEVER sends it (verified: runTurn call omits it) — so today neither backend exposes constrained final-message output through the stock adapters; acp-agents must add it or keep client-side validation.

openDecisions.decision: Usage delivery: keep usage out-of-band via the onUsage callback, or fold it into a richer AgentResult wrapper.
openDecisions.recommendation: Keep onUsage out-of-band. The engine call site (workflow.ts:482-484) already depends on the closure-var callback form; wrapping would force a change to the frozen seam call. Freeze AgentResult = raw value (string|Static<schema>) + onUsage(AgentUsage) callback.

openDecisions.decision: Should options.agent be REQUIRED (drop the `?? new WorkflowAgent(options)` Pi default at workflow.ts:283)?
openDecisions.recommendation: Make it required (or default to a thrown-if-missing factory). Dropping the Pi default is the single line that de-couples workflow-engine from any agent implementation; the README states the engine depends on an injected AgentRunner, not on acp-agents.

openDecisions.decision: Schema type at the seam: keep typebox TSchema/Static, or abstract to a generic/JSON-Schema interface.
options: Carry JSON Schema alongside, hash a canonical form
openDecisions.recommendation: Keep typebox for the freeze: it is JSON.stringify'd into the resume hash (journal compatibility) and keeps extractValidated/Convert/Check verbatim. Typebox already compiles to JSON Schema, which is what Claude outputFormat and Codex outputSchema both want downstream, so it is forward-compatible.

openDecisions.decision: Where do AgentRunOptions/AgentRunResult/AgentUsage/AgentHistoryEntry live in the 3-package split.
openDecisions.recommendation: A dedicated shared-types package imported by both workflow-engine and acp-agents. WorkflowAgentOptions (currently extended by WorkflowRunOptions) becomes an engine-local type.

openDecisions.decision: Model resolution ownership: engine pre-resolves a concrete spec vs runner interprets the model/tier strings.
openDecisions.recommendation: Keep the current split: engine resolves tier->phase->model to a spec STRING (resolveModelForPhase/resolveAgentModelSpec) and passes both model and tier; the runner does final interpretation and reports the actual id via onModelResolved / falls back via onModelFallback. Over ACP, model is not a first-class wire field — the runner maps the spec to which ACP server to spawn (cross-provider) or a session/set_config_option / Claude _meta.claudeCode.options.model (within-provider).

openDecisions.decision: toolNames/disallowedToolNames semantics over ACP (Pi coding-tool allow/deny lists have no direct ACP equivalent).
options: Keep fields, map to ACP permission decisions
openDecisions.recommendation: Keep the fields in AgentRunOptions (engine populates from agentDef) and document them as permission-policy hints that the runner maps onto session/request_permission auto-responses / permission mode. Do not rename for the freeze — renaming would churn the engine call site and agentDef plumbing.

openDecisions.decision: maxSchemaRetries exposure: leaf-internal default vs surfaced to engine/MCP tool.
options: Plumb to engine + MCP tool input
openDecisions.recommendation: Keep it in AgentRunOptions with the leaf default of 2; do not add it to the workflow MCP tool envelope for the freeze (no demand, and the engine does not pass it today).

openDecisions.decision: Structured-output result channel in acp-agents (how the runner produces Static<schema>).
openDecisions.recommendation: Backend-native-constraint -> client-side validate -> re-prompt ladder (README §6.5). Port extractValidated and resolveStructuredOutput verbatim into acp-agents; fill StructuredOutputCapture from the native result (Claude structured_output off raw _claude/sdkMessage; Codex structuredContent) instead of a tool execute(). run()'s return contract is unchanged.

openDecisions.decision: Workflow MCP tool bounds (concurrency<=16, agentRetries<=3): reject via Zod .max or silently clamp in the handler.
openDecisions.recommendation: Silent clamp in the handler to match the README wording 'clamped to 16'. Zod .max() REJECTS rather than clamps, so encoding the bound in the schema would change observable behavior; clamp + document, and keep only type/positivity validation in Zod.

openDecisions.decision: Workflow MCP tool: declare an outputSchema (forcing structuredContent + SDK validation) or return text only.
openDecisions.recommendation: Declare a minimal outputSchema for the run result (runId, status, result, tokenUsage, optionally logs) so clients get a validated structured payload, plus a human-readable text content block. This pins the WorkflowRunResult shared type.

openDecisions.decision: checkpoint() fallback when the MCP host lacks the elicitation.form capability (server.elicitInput THROWS, it does not silently fall back).
openDecisions.recommendation: Catch the elicitInput throw and apply the engine's existing headless default (checkpointOptions.default ?? true, workflow.ts:828) — i.e. auto-proceed. This matches current headless semantics and avoids a hard dependency on host elicitation support.

openDecisions.decision: Progress propagation from engine to MCP shell.
openDecisions.recommendation: Add an onProgress callback to the engine run options; the MCP shell maps it to extra.sendNotification({method:'notifications/progress', params:{progressToken,...}}) and tolerates a missing client progressToken (skip if absent).

gaps.what: ACP SDK subscribe model: does registering an app-level onNotification('session/update') handler AND using the ActiveSession pump double-deliver updates for the same sessionId?
gaps.why: Affects how the runner drains assistant text/usage/history; double-delivery would corrupt history and token accounting.
gaps.howToResolve: Read dist/acp.js (impl, not just .d.ts) for ActiveSession's private routing/registrations; standardize on ONE mechanism (the ActiveSession.nextUpdate() pump) for the runner. Non-blocking for freezing run(prompt,opts)->result.

gaps.what: Mapping of ACP session/prompt + session/new errors (auth_required, RequestError codes) to run() rejections / WorkflowError recoverability.
gaps.why: The engine's retry/pause behavior depends on .recoverable and .code; provider-quota must become a recoverable PROVIDER_USAGE_LIMIT checkpoint, auth must surface distinctly.
gaps.howToResolve: Read jsonrpc.d.ts RequestError statics (requestCancelled/authRequired/etc.) and the agent-side auth path; define a provider-error -> WorkflowError(code,recoverable,resetHint) map in acp-agents.

gaps.what: Whether real agents actually populate PromptResponse.usage (it is marked UNSTABLE/experimental).
gaps.why: Determines whether AgentUsage can be filled from the standard ACP path or must be sourced from a side-channel; total===0 forces the engine's chars/4 estimate.
gaps.howToResolve: Confirm claude-agent-acp emits usage (raw _claude/sdkMessage result + usage_update) and codex-acp surfaces SessionState.lastTokenUsage/totalTokenUsage; wire whichever is reliable into onUsage on both success and error paths.

gaps.what: Claude outputFormat is session-scoped/init-only (no Query.setOutputFormat); does a re-prompt within the same session during resolveStructuredOutput retries still validate against the same schema?
gaps.why: If the schema constraint is not re-applied on retry turns, the runner must create a fresh query/session per schema, changing the runner's session lifecycle.
gaps.howToResolve: Confirm in claude-agent-acp/@anthropic-ai SDK whether retry turns retain outputFormat; if not, runner spawns a new session when opts.schema changes.

gaps.what: Does the downstream Codex app-server honor TurnStartParams.outputSchema (the field exists but codex-acp never sends it, and the Codex binary is out of tree)?
gaps.why: Decides whether the Codex runner can constrain the final message natively or must rely on client-side prose extraction.
gaps.howToResolve: Out-of-tree: either (a) thread opts.outputSchema into runTurn and test against a real Codex binary in the implementation phase, or (b) default to client-side extractValidated. No runtime evidence available from codex-acp alone.

gaps.what: Source of the 'available models' list for buildDefaultTierConfig once agent.ts is replaced (model-tier-config.ts:18 imports listAvailableModelSpecs from agent.ts, transitively pulling Pi).
gaps.why: model-tier-config.ts lifts into workflow-engine but currently has a Pi-transitive import that must be cut.
gaps.howToResolve: Source available models from the ACP NewSessionResponse.configOptions ('model' select options) per provider; cut/redirect the listAvailableModelSpecs import during the port.

gaps.what: agentType registry source directory is hardcoded to .pi/agents and ~/.pi/agents (workflow.ts:260,372); agentDefinitionKey feeds the resume hash.
gaps.why: The dir must be parameterized for a Pi-independent engine, but the key FORMAT must stay byte-stable or existing journals break.
gaps.howToResolve: Parameterize the agents directory while keeping agentDefinitionKey's serialization unchanged so hashAgentCall remains journal-compatible.

gaps.what: Residual Pi imports in files marked 'lift unchanged': structured-output.ts still imports defineTool from @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent; the demotion must drop it.
gaps.why: Leaves a Pi dependency in a package that is supposed to be Pi-free.
gaps.howToResolve: On port, drop the defineTool import; keep only the StructuredOutputCapture cell pattern, filled from the native structured result rather than a tool execute().

gaps.what: ACP config-option method name spelling (README uses both session/setConfigOption camelCase and session/set_config_option snake_case).
gaps.why: Wrong literal silently breaks mid-session model/permission changes.
gaps.howToResolve: Grep the on-disk ACP SDK dist/schema/index.d.ts for the exact AGENT_METHODS literal and standardize Phase-1 code on it (the SDK uses snake_case method strings).

discrepanciesWithReadme: README l114-115/497: AgentRunner.run is 'used at :283' — WRONG (verified). workflow.ts:283 is the BINDING `const agentRunner = options.agent ?? new WorkflowAgent(options);`. The ONLY .run() call is workflow.ts:465, inside the limiter thunk in agent(), wrapped by withTimeout. Phase 1 should treat :465 as the seam call and :283 as the seam binding.

README l215: checkpoint headless default cited at workflow.ts:819 — :819 is the start of the `if (options.confirm)` branch; the actual headless-default literal is :828 (`reply = checkpointOptions.default ?? true`). checkpoint() is defined at :798. Minor.

README l135: pipeline labeled ':579, no barrier' — pipeline DOES join all items via Promise.all at workflow.ts:588 (a cross-item barrier). 'no barrier' is accurate only inter-STAGE (each item flows through its stages sequentially). Clarify so a porter does not drop the Promise.all.
agent.ts size: README §2/header say ~540 lines; the actual file is 496 lines. Content matches; cosmetic drift.

README/task description said session/new carries configOptions ('session/new (cwd, mcpServers, configOptions)') — FALSE (verified). NewSessionRequest is { cwd, additionalDirectories?, mcpServers (required), _meta? }. configOptions are RETURNED in NewSessionResponse and mutated via session/set_config_option (SetSessionConfigOptionRequest). SessionBuilder has no withConfigOption.

README/task description said connection.d.ts holds the Connection/Client/Agent classes/interfaces — FALSE. connection.d.ts is the HTTP/multiplexing layer only; the Client interface (acp.d.ts:1322-1462), Agent interface (1469-1774), and ClientSideConnection/AgentSideConnection live in acp.d.ts.

README §5.4 uses two spellings for the model-switch method: session/setConfigOption (camelCase) and session/set_config_option (snake_case). The ACP-standard literal is snake_case; the camelCase form is a README typo. Flag for Phase-1 ACP verification.

README l199-200: concurrency 'clamped to 16' / agentRetries '<=3' — the MCP SDK does NOT clamp. Zod .max() REJECTS (InvalidParams), it does not silently clamp. README wording 'clamped' implies a silent clamp, which differs from Zod's reject behavior. Decide reject-vs-clamp in Phase 1.

README l214-216: checkpoint() 'falls back to ... elicitation' — only true when the MCP host advertises the elicitation.form capability. server.elicitInput THROWS otherwise (server/index.js:158-161,362). The README overstates; checkpoint() needs its own fallback when elicitation is unavailable.

claude-agent-acp central nuance (README gap, not a line drift): the adapter NEVER reads SDKResultSuccess.structured_output; the success-subtype handler (acp-agent.ts:1704-1738) forwards only message.result text. structured_output is surfaced ONLY via the raw _claude/sdkMessage ext-notification (requires emitRawSDKMessages + outputFormat in _meta.claudeCode.options). This is the central rough edge for the structured-output rebuild. All README-cited line numbers in claude-agent-acp ARE exact for the b8df8e0e checkout (no line drift).

codex-acp: README cited v2 TurnStartParams.outputSchema at ~:43-46; the actual field `outputSchema?: JsonValue|null` is on line 45 (doc comment 42-44). Minor off-by-a-couple. Confirmed (verified): the codex-acp adapter NEVER populates outputSchema in its runTurn call, so per-turn structured output is not wired through codex-acp today.

codex-acp: the 'config options temporarily disabled' caveat exists ONLY as a source comment (CodexAcpServer.ts:805, LLM-28118) and is gated to JetBrains 2026.1 clients (isSessionConfigEnabled = !isJetBrains2026_1Client); it is NOT globally disabled and NOT mentioned in README/readme-dev. Also: v1 SendUserTurnParams is exported but dead code — the server speaks v2 'turn/start' only.

ACP ndJsonStream(output: WritableStream, input: ReadableStream) parameter order is (writable-to-agent first, readable-from-agent second), but the SDK's own example uses local var names that invert this (input=Writable.toWeb(stdin), output=Readable.toWeb(stdout)). Positionally correct but a porting hazard for the stdio client wiring (not a README discrepancy, but a correction to the SDK example).
readiness.summary: READY TO FREEZE — proceed to Phase 1. The AgentRunner seam and the shared types are fully pinned by verbatim reads of the actual engine source, and I independently re-verified the most load-bearing facts: the seam field `agent?: Pick<WorkflowAgent,\"run\">` (workflow.ts:59), the binding-vs-call split (:283 binds, :465 is the only call), and AgentUsage/AgentRunOptions/AgentRunResult (agent.ts:225-288). Two separate readers covering the same engine files agree exactly, and the downstream ACP/Claude/Codex/MCP surfaces are consistent and high-confidence. I also re-verified the key downstream constraints: ACP core has ZERO native structured output (grep of all dist/*.d.ts = 0), PromptResponse carries only stopReason+optional(UNSTABLE) usage with no content field, NewSessionRequest has no configOptions, and the codex-acp adapter never sends turn outputSchema. The freezable contract: AgentRunner = single-method { run(prompt:string, opts:AgentRunOptions<S>): Promise<AgentRunResult<S>> }; AgentResult is the RAW value (string when no schema, Static<schema> otherwise) with usage delivered out-of-band via onUsage(AgentUsage{input,output,cacheRead,cacheWrite,total,cost}); errors thrown as WorkflowError{code,recoverable}; timeout/abort owned by the engine; result must JSON-round-trip for journal/resume. The workflow MCP tool input envelope (script + 7 optional knobs) and output (CallToolResult with optional structuredContent) are likewise pinned, with a synchronous one-call-per-run model and notifications/progress as the only in-flight channel. Every remaining item is either (a) a genuine DESIGN decision that freezing is meant to RESOLVE — all with clear, low-risk recommendations grounded in current engine behavior (keep usage out-of-band, keep typebox at the seam, make options.agent required, shared-types package, silent-clamp the MCP bounds, elicitation fallback to default-true) — or (b) a NON-blocking implementation gap in the acp-agents runner (ACP error mapping, usage population, Claude session-scoped outputFormat retries, Codex outputSchema honoring, registry dir parameterization) that does NOT change the shape of run(prompt,opts)->result. The 12 README discrepancies are minor: line-citation drift (:283 vs :465, :819 vs :828), one structural mislabel (pipeline's Promise.all barrier), and several task-description errors about ACP (configOptions on session/new, connection.d.ts contents) — none alter the contract semantics. Freeze the interface and shared types now; defer the runner-internal gaps to the implementation phase.
